# Chapter 12: Automated Data Collection Methods — Knowledge Points

## 1. Core Concept Definitions

**Automated Data Collection** refers to the use of software tools to collect vast amounts of user interaction data with little or no direct effort on the part of the researcher. These methods range from existing commodity software (web logs) to system observation tools (activity loggers) to custom-built research software (instrumented applications). The approaches lie on a spectrum of ease of use versus flexibility.

**Web Logs** are files generated by web servers, email servers, and database servers that store records of requests and activity. They provide a sequential listing of all requests made to a server, including host IP address, timestamp, HTTP method, requested resource, protocol version, status code, size, referrer, and user agent. Web logs are a potent tool for HCI research, enabling usability evaluation, usage pattern analysis, and empirical task performance studies.

**Common Log Format** is the standard format used by most web servers to format log files. It includes fields for host address, timestamp, HTTP request details, status code, and size. Web servers can be customized to include additional fields or create special-purpose log files for specific research needs.

**Web Proxy** is an intermediary server that receives all web requests from a group of users, retrieves requested materials, and returns them to users. Unlike standard web server logs that only capture requests to a single site, web proxies capture all requests from all users across many websites. Web proxies can intercept and modify both user requests and server responses.

**Stored Application Data** refers to traces left by computer use that provide information about how users interact with applications and manage information. Examples include file system structures, GUI desktop arrangements, email folder organizations, web bookmarks, and social networking connections. This data is collected automatically by the tools users work with daily.

**Activity-Logging Software** runs invisibly in the background, recording mouse movements, keyboard input, and windowing system interactions including window movement, resizing, opening, and closing. These tools act as proxies for user interaction events, recording them as they happen before passing them to applications or the operating environment.

**Keystroke Logger** is a special subclass of activity-logging software that focuses only on keyboard input. While valuable for research, these tools have achieved notoriety as "spyware" when used to surreptitiously record user interactions.

**Web Proxy (Research-Oriented)** differs from caching proxies in that it serves as a research intermediary. The proxy captures complete session data for all users, providing a broader picture of user activities than standard server logs. Research proxies like WebQuilt combine logging with visualization and analysis capabilities.

**Instrumented Software** refers to applications with added measurement and recording tools to track usage. Constructing instrumented software requires modifying source code to handle user interactions and record them. Approaches include using macro/extension facilities in commercial products, modifying open-source software, or adding instrumentation to commercial products with vendor cooperation.

**Interaction Recording Tools** capture screen video and potentially microphone audio, providing a record of what happened and when. They range from commodity screen recorders to dedicated usability study packages like TechSmith's Morae that augment basic capture with mouse/keyboard logging and webcam integration.

**Custom Research Software** is software explicitly created for running experiments, presenting users with tasks and recording completion time, errors, and other relevant data. Examples include Fitts' Law studies measuring mouse movement accuracy.

**Granularity** refers to the amount of detail in collected data. Fine-grained, high-resolution data involves every possible user interaction event (individual keystrokes, mouse movements). Coarse-grained, low-resolution data contains fewer events (menu selections, button clicks, dialog interactions). The appropriate granularity depends on research questions.

**Hybrid Data Collection** combines multiple channels of data collection to overcome shortcomings of any single approach. Examples include combining log files with video recordings, proxies with instrumented browsers, or automated capture with human observation. Software tools can synchronize these data streams for integrated analysis.

**Parsing** is the process of reading log files one line at a time, breaking each entry into constituent pieces, and updating data structures that keep counts and statistics of different event types. Parsers are often written in scripting languages like Perl and Python.

**Fitts' Law** states that the time required to move between targets is a function of the logarithm of the ratio of movement amplitude to target width. As distance between targets increases or target size decreases, movement time increases. This has profound implications for interface design.

**Data Mining** involves extracting patterns from large data sets using techniques such as association rules and clustering. In HCI, data mining can identify frequently co-occurring user actions, cluster users into groups based on usage patterns, and predict outcomes such as purchases.

## 2. Theoretical Frameworks

**Ease-of-Use vs. Flexibility Spectrum**: Automated data collection approaches exist on a spectrum from easy-to-use but limited (existing software tools) to flexible but labor-intensive (custom-built software). Web logs are simplest but may be limited in the data collected. Activity loggers are more powerful but require configuration. Custom software can meet precise research needs but requires substantial development effort.

**Three Categories of Research Questions**: Automated data collection addresses three broad categories: (1) retrospective analyses of information management behavior (examining artifacts of computer use), (2) controlled experiments (collecting timing and related data using web logs or customized software), and (3) usability studies and explorations of how users work with tools.

**Granularity-Analysis Trade-off**: The amount of data collected (granularity) must match research questions. Fine-grained data (every mouse movement) is overwhelming for long-term usage studies. Coarse-grained data (application-level usage) is insufficient for understanding specific interaction patterns. Tools should ideally allow flexible granularity with events marked by level.

**Proxies vs. Direct Instrumentation**: Proxies intercept interactions transparently without modifying the application, providing broad coverage but limited detail. Direct instrumentation modifies the application to capture specific events, providing precise data but requiring access to source code. Each approach has tradeoffs in coverage, precision, and implementation effort.

**Automated Inspection Model**: Automated inspection tools analyze interface layout, content, and language to determine guideline compliance. While useful for identifying obvious issues, these tools should complement—not replace—traditional design reviews and user testing. Their utility is limited by the validity and scope of underlying guidelines.

**Ecological Validity in Automated Collection**: Automated methods can collect data in natural settings with minimal researcher interference, potentially increasing ecological validity compared to laboratory observations. However, the lack of contextual knowledge about user goals means log data alone may be insufficient for understanding the "why" behind observed behaviors.

## 3. Methodology Steps

### Step 1: Identify Research Questions and Data Needs

Before collecting data, precisely identify what you hope to learn and how collected data will help answer those questions. Determine the appropriate level of granularity. Consider whether you need individual keystrokes, menu selections, application-level usage, or long-term usage patterns. Collecting too much, too little, or the wrong data wastes resources.

### Step 2: Select Appropriate Data Collection Tools

Choose tools based on research needs along the ease-of-use/flexibility spectrum:
- **Existing software**: Use when default data collection is sufficient (web logs, stored application data)
- **Activity loggers**: Use when you need broader interaction tracking across applications
- **Web proxies**: Use when you need to track user activity across multiple websites
- **Instrumented software**: Use when you need specific data not captured by existing tools
- **Custom software**: Use when you need complete control over data collection

### Step 3: Configure Data Collection

For web logs: configure server to include necessary fields, consider creating special-purpose logs, use robot exclusion protocol for public sites, and disable browser caching during experiments. For proxies: ensure sufficient processing power and network bandwidth, configure browser settings or router-level proxies. For instrumented software: modify source code to record events of interest, associate recorded actions with categories for ease of analysis. For all methods: start each session with an empty log file, move completed session logs to separate directories.

### Step 4: Address Privacy and Ethical Concerns

Implement privacy protections before collecting data. Consider anonymizing IP addresses, removing identifying information from logs, limiting data collection to what is strictly necessary, and destroying data when analysis is complete. Ensure websites have privacy policies explaining data collection. For proxy-based studies, be aware that users may forget their requests are being logged. Check whether IRB approval is needed.

### Step 5: Conduct Data Collection

Run the study with proper controls. For empirical studies using web logs: present tasks as web links, use local server to minimize network delays, record start and end events via link selections. For activity logging studies: ensure logging software is properly configured, verify antispyware tools won't interfere, provide participants with clear consent forms.

### Step 6: Parse and Analyze Log Files

Write or use existing parsers (often in Perl or Python) to read log files line by line, extract relevant fields, and compute statistics. Use established web log analysis tools (Google Analytics for public sites, or specialized tools for research). For non-web applications, consider formatting logs to match web server formats for tool compatibility. Use visualization tools (like WebQuilt) to display usage patterns as graphs.

### Step 7: Apply Advanced Analysis Techniques

For complex questions, employ sequence analysis to study event ordering, data mining to extract patterns, and machine learning to classify user sessions. Visualize data at multiple granularities using interactive tools. Consider combining automated data with qualitative observation for richer understanding.

### Step 8: Manage Data Storage

Choose between log files (simple text files, easy to write) and databases (powerful queries with SQL, useful when application already uses a database). Design data storage to facilitate analysis. For database-driven web applications, adding user activity tables is straightforward.

## 4. Decision Criteria

**When to Use Web Logs**: Use for studying web-based interactions, site usability evaluation, and empirical studies involving web-link selections. Web logs are ideal for understanding which pages are accessed, how users navigate between pages, and when interactions occur. Limitations include lack of contextual knowledge about user goals and inability to track client-side JavaScript interactions.

**When to Use Web Proxies**: Use when you need to track user activity across multiple websites, not just a single site. Proxies provide complete session data but require significant technical setup, processing power, and bandwidth. Consider for studies of general browsing habits and multi-site usage patterns.

**When to Use Activity Loggers**: Use for studying multi-application workflows, application switching patterns, and long-term usage studies. Activity loggers capture interactions at the operating system level, providing broader coverage than application-specific tools. Consider privacy implications carefully.

**When to Use Instrumented Software**: Use when existing tools don't capture the specific data you need. Particularly valuable for studying complex application usage patterns. Consider the effort required for modification and whether macro/extension facilities or open-source code is available.

**When to Use Custom Software**: Use for structured experiments requiring precise control over task presentation and data collection. Most appropriate for Fitts' Law studies, menu comparison experiments, and other controlled investigations.

**Granularity Selection**: Match data granularity to research questions:
- Long-term usage patterns: coarse-grained (application-level, feature-level)
- Task completion studies: medium-grained (menu selections, button clicks)
- Detailed interaction analysis: fine-grained (keystrokes, mouse movements)
- Consider tools that support multiple granularity levels with event tagging

**Hybrid vs. Single-Method**: Use hybrid approaches when single methods cannot provide sufficient data. Combine log data with video for contextual understanding, proxies with instrumented browsers for comprehensive coverage, or automated capture with human observation for richer interpretation.

## 5. Book Cases and Examples

**Simultaneous vs. Sequential Menus Study**: A comparison of web menu structures for accessing US Census Bureau economic data. A sequential menu allowed users to select county → business category → year, while simultaneous menus allowed selection in any order. Using locally hosted web server with standard log files, researchers found that sequential menus fared well for simple tasks, but simultaneous menus were preferable for more complex tasks. Task completion times were extracted from log timestamps.

**WebQuilt**: A proxy server specifically designed for usability data collection. WebQuilt combines logging with transformation of log entries into inferred user actions, aggregation into graph structures, and visualization components. Unlike general-purpose proxies, WebQuilt handles site-specific data collection without requiring browser configuration, modifying pages to route requests through the proxy.

**MouseTracks and UsaProxy**: JavaScript-based systems that modified web pages with code recording low-level interaction data including mouse movements. This data was sent to servers for logging and visualization, providing finer-grained data than standard proxies.

**Microsoft Office 2003 Instrumentation**: Microsoft's Customer Experience Improvement Program collected anonymous usage data from over 13 billion user sessions. Data included menu selections, keyboard shortcuts, and customization artifacts. Key finding: the Paste command was the most popular (11% of all Word command usage), and the Paste button was the most frequently clicked toolbar button. This informed the redesign of Office 2007's interface. Also confirmed that top five Word commands accounted for over 32% of all usage.

**Personalized Interface Study (McGrenere et al.)**: Used scripting tools in Microsoft Word 2000 to build an extension allowing users to work with simplified, personalized interfaces. A field study with 20 users installed logging tools capturing usage histograms. Results showed only a small number of commands were used very frequently, and users added almost all of those to their personalized interfaces.

**Ingimp (Instrumented GIMP)**: An instrumented version of the GNU Image Manipulation Program that collected usage timing, window/layer counts, command usage, and task-switching details. Privacy measures included not recording specific key presses or mouse positions, providing a startup dialog to disable logging, and making all source code available for inspection.

**Fitts' Law Children's Mouse Study**: Custom software tracked mouse movement accuracy for 13 four-year-olds, 13 five-year-olds, and 13 adults. Participants moved between targets of varying sizes (16, 32, 64 pixels) at varying distances (128, 256, 512 pixels). Children were slower, less accurate, and exhibited more target hovering and reentry. Mouse motion paths showed children moving far afield while adults moved accurately.

**DART (Disruption and Recovery Tracker)**: Logged window positions, sizes, actions, and alerts while limiting keyboard logging to menu shortcuts and punctuation. Analysis of 2200+ hours from 27 people generated insights into time lost due to email/IM alerts and user recovery patterns. Exemplified responsible data collection with privacy protection.

**LAB-IN-A-BOX**: Combined directional audio, eye tracking, screen capture, mouse movements, and Kinect body orientation data for studying electronic medical records during patient visits. Configured in a rolling case for 10-minute setup. Used ChronoViz for synchronized multi-stream analysis, described as "Computational Ethnography."

## 6. Common Errors and Pitfalls

**Collecting Too Much Data**: The temptation to collect everything "just in case" leads to overwhelming data volumes that are difficult to analyze. Even with storage being inexpensive, sifting through megabytes of event logs to find meaningful patterns is challenging. Design data collection around specific research questions.

**Collecting Too Little Data**: Conversely, collecting insufficient data or data at the wrong granularity may fail to answer research questions. Fine-grained data is needed for detailed interaction studies; coarse-grained data suffices for long-term patterns.

**Ignoring Privacy Implications**: Activity loggers can capture sensitive information including document titles, email addresses, and web browsing habits. Web proxies track all requests including potentially embarrassing sites. IP addresses can identify specific computers and potentially individuals. Always implement privacy protections, obtain informed consent, and consider IRB approval.

**Not Accounting for Browser Caching**: Browser caches store local copies of recently accessed pages. Requests for cached pages don't generate server log entries, potentially causing incomplete data. Disable caching during controlled experiments.

**Confusing Correlation with Causation in Log Analysis**: Long intervals between page requests might indicate careful reading OR distraction. Repeated cycling between help and search pages might indicate task failure OR deliberate exploration. Log data lacks contextual knowledge about user goals; combine with observation or interviews.

**Robot Contamination of Web Logs**: Publicly accessible websites receive requests from web robots (search engine crawlers). These automated tools follow links and retrieve pages, contaminating log data. Use the robot exclusion protocol for short periods during data collection.

**Ignoring Session Identification Challenges**: Web logs may contain interleaved requests from multiple users. Firewalls make requests from different users appear as coming from the same machine. Identifying individual user sessions from log files is imperfect. Cookies may be disabled. Browser configuration can provide misleading user agent and referrer information.

**Inadequate Proxy Server Capacity**: If a proxy server cannot handle the processing and bandwidth demands, users will experience browsing delays, potentially changing their habits or refusing to use the proxy. This biases data collection. Ensure dedicated, adequate resources.

**Not Validating Automated Data Collection**: Failing to pilot test data collection and analysis tools before full deployment may result in collecting unusable data. Verify that collected data actually tells you what you want it to.

**Over-reliance on Automated Tools**: Automated inspection tools for interface evaluation should complement—not replace—traditional design reviews and user testing. Their utility is limited by the validity of underlying guidelines.

**Not Documenting Instrumentation Decisions**: When instrumenting software, failing to document which events are recorded, at what granularity, and with what privacy protections makes it difficult to interpret results or replicate studies.

**Ignoring Higher-Level Task Inference Challenges**: Low-level interaction events (keystrokes, mouse clicks) don't directly reveal higher-level tasks (formatting text, inserting a table). Inferring tasks from interaction data requires domain knowledge and pattern recognition, which is imperfect. Applications with multiple paths to the same function complicate analysis.

**Not Combining Automated Data with Qualitative Context**: Log files provide detailed records of what happened and when, but lack information about why users acted as they did. Combining automated data with observation, interviews, or think-aloud protocols provides much richer understanding.
